questions.description: The role of fault in the allocation of tort liability has outlived its…
questions.text: The role of fault in the allocation of tort liability has outlived its usefulness in the modern world, particularly with new emerging technologies. Critically discuss this statement in light of the role and function of tort law in the modern legal system.

questions.description: Sam was part of a protest walking down the main street. Advise Diane if she has any action in tort against Sam on these…
questions.text: Sam was part of a protest walking down the main street. He was protesting the continuation of the mandatory requirement to wear facemasks in certain situations. He felt this was an affront to the freedom which his forebears had fought and died for. The protest was peaceful although none of the protestors were wearing facemasks. However, Sam's protestors were shortly joined by another group of lawful protestors who were expressing the opposite opinion: they wanted mandatory wearing of facemasks in all situations where there were a large number of people. One of these protestors, Diane, who was wearing a disposable mask began harassing Sam, shouting at him that he needed to believe in the science, that no one was taking away his freedoms and so on. She also shouted that it was people like him who had caused the death of her grandfather from Covid. Sam just kept singing the national anthem at her. As she became angrier, her shouting became louder and at that point her own face mask broke and fell to the ground. Seeing his chance, Sam turned to Diane and sang directly in her face. When he had finished, he told her that he had Covid symptoms but was not going to get tested but he hoped that he did have it. He finished by saying "with luck, you will catch it as well and see it for the fake news that it is!" Diane was hysterical at this and ran away. Advise Diane if she has any action in tort against Sam on these facts.

questions.description: Edwina is an environmentalist who lives in a remote part of the country… Advise John if he has any action in nuisance…
questions.text: Edwina is an environmentalist who lives in a remote part of the country where she has decided to try to live a minimalist lifestyle. She abhors any use of fossil fuels and now wants to disconnect from the electricity network because she believes it relies too heavily on non-renewable energy sources. Instead, she has decided to generate her own electricity using wind power. As a result of this, Edwina has installed a mini windmill in her back garden. To cater for those periods where this is insufficient to provide for her needs, she has a diesel generator which automatically kicks in to provide electricity. She hopes however that this will only be used as a last resort. Unfortunately, climate change has meant that where Edwina lives the wind either blows very strongly or not at all. This has meant that the diesel generator is now being used for a longer period of time to cover Edwina's electricity needs. John, her next-door neighbor, is complaining about the noise that the diesel generator makes and also that there is a significant diesel smell from the generator when it is in operation. So much so that he finds it difficult to sleep and feels he cannot use his garden when it is on due to the smell it gives off. In addition, during high winds, John believes that the windmill causes a vibrational hum in John's house. Advise John if he has any action in nuisance under tort law in these circumstances.

questions.description: Vincent loves vintage cars and has recently restored a 1969 Caprica to… Advise Maurice as to who is the cause of the…
questions.text: Vincent loves vintage cars and has recently restored a 1969 Caprica to pristine condition. Many of the original parts were not available and he had to have them made specifically to order. For that he found Maurice, a supplier who, when given the drawing of the required part, would make them in his engineering workshop. One day Vincent was out driving his 1969 Caprica downtown. In front of him was Joshua, a rival vintage car restorer who was envious of Vincent's Caprica restoration. Seeking to give Vincent a fright, Joshua stopped his car suddenly and without warning. Unfortunately, Joshua's brake lights were not working, and Vincent pressed the brakes hard but too late. He crashed into the back of Joshua's car. As a result of the impact, a pipe taking petrol from the petrol tank to the engine became dislodged. That pipe had been made by Maurice by copying the old part that Vincent had supplied. In fact that pipe had been made by the previous owner and was entirely unsuitable for that purpose. As the pipe dislodged, petrol spilled from the Caprica and seeped underneath several parked cars. Meghan, who was watching the events unfold, decided she would see if either Vincent or Joshua needed help. As she began to run to the incident, she threw her cigarette on the ground unaware that the petrol had been leaking. The cigarette set light to the petrol and destroyed the parked vehicles. Advise Maurice as to who is the cause of the damage to the parked cars under the law of tort.

questions.description: Critically discuss the reform of defamation law paying particular attention to the need to protect the reputation of…
questions.text: Critically discuss the reform of defamation law paying particular attention to the need to protect the reputation of plaintiffs.

questions.description: Brendan was a yacht salesperson, who worked for Yachts Ltd. Advise Yachts Ltd if they are liable in tort law for the…
questions.text: Brendan was a yacht salesperson, who worked for Yachts Ltd. His job was to take these yachts around to various different marinas in the country and then show potential customers what a great time could be had by inviting potential customers onto the boat to experience the lifestyle that goes with a yacht. Brendan was given considerable control over what he could do so long as he made the sale. The only limitation that Yachts Ltd placed on their salesperson was that they were not allowed to use the yacht for their personal use but only to use the yacht for a potential sale. One week Brendan was at a marina, and he felt that business was very slow. He was trying to encourage people to take the boat for a test spin to see if they would be interested in buying it, but there were no takers. As the day drew to a close, Brendan had a brainwave. He decided that he would invite a number of his friends, a cool crowd of interesting people, to a party on the boat that night, hoping that people, seeing the fun that was being had on the yacht, would then decide to visit and arrange a test spin for the following day. As the party was in full swing, Bill dropped by and said he was interested in taking the yacht for a test spin. Brendan asked that he return the next day, but that tonight was for partying. Bill was disappointed and many of Brendan's friends also began to encourage Brendan to take the yacht out for a spin. Eventually, Brendan relented and took the yacht out into the bay where it collided with another yacht due to Brendan's fault. Advise Yachts Ltd if they are liable in tort law for the actions of Brendan under these circumstances.

questions.description: Critically evaluate the classification of damages in tort actions and in particular the role of punitive and exemplary…
questions.text: Critically evaluate the classification of damages in tort actions and in particular the role of punitive and exemplary damages.

questions.description: Miriam owned Rover, a pet Labrador since he was a puppy. Advise Miriam if she has any action in the law of tort against…
questions.text: Miriam owned Rover, a pet Labrador since he was a puppy. However, Miriam was unable to train Rover and as he grew, she found it more and more difficult to control him on the lead. Rover was a heavy and powerful dog which often led to Miriam being forced to drop his lead and leaving him to run free until he eventually came back of his own accord. Rover was well known in Miriam's neighborhood as was Miriam's inability to control him on the lead. One day while Miriam was taking Rover for a walk, Rover spotted a cat in one of the fields and he broke free from Miriam. He began chasing the cat onto the road. Fearful of Rover being hit by a passing car, Miriam began screaming for someone to help. Vanessa, who had been out jogging heard Miriam and saw Rover running into the road. Realising what had happened, Vanessa raced after Rover and began shouting at him, calling him to come to her. As Rover was still too interested in chasing the cat, Vanessas realised that the best thing she could do would be to lure Rover to her with a sweet that was in her back pocket. When Rover saw Vanessa with the sweet, he stopped, and the cat ran back into the field. Rover looked at the cat and then at Vanessa before deciding to head towards the sweet. As Rover ran toward Vanessa, he was struck by a car and seriously injured. Miriam has paid thousands of euros to the vet for Rover's treatment. Advise Miriam if she has any action in the law of tort against Vanessa based on these facts.

questions.description: Jacqueline owns a small country hotel that has only just re-opened after… Advise Jacqueline of her legal position with…
questions.text: Jacqueline owns a small country hotel that has only just re-opened after the pandemic. Business is slow and with an impending cost of living crisis, she is concerned that she may have to close permanently. Fred, who owns a plot of land next to her hotel, has begun organising open air musical events which attract large crowds. At first, Jacqueline hopes that those going to the events will come to her hotel and either stay the night or at least buy drinks from the bar. But this does not happen, although she does find that many of those attending the event are spilling out from Fred's field and partying on her land. Jacqueline then decides to put up signs stating: trespassers will be prosecuted. However, she does not up any fencing on the land itself to stop it from happening. Instead, she sets up a van on the dividing line between her and Fred's land which sells drinks and food and carries the name of the hotel. She finds that many people think they can use the restrooms of her hotel, but she turns them away and reminds them it is private property, and they need to leave. One night, Sally was attending one of the events at Fred's. Having bought some drink at the van, she walked up to the hotel seeking to use the restroom, but Jacqueline turned her away saying she was a trespasser. On the way back, Sally saw a pond just outside the hotel and decided it would be fun to jump into it. Unfortunately, someone had thrown rubbish into the pond which was not visible in the dark. When Sally jumped in, her foot got caught in the rubbish and broke her leg. Advise Jacqueline of her legal position with respect to these events in the law of tort.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law relating to defective products and whether the current legal framework provides a suitable…
questions.text: Critically discuss the law relating to defective products and whether the current legal framework provides a suitable response in the modern world.

questions.description: Alan owns a bakery that makes a product called The Good Kake. Advise Alan what action, if any, he might have against…
questions.text: Alan owns a bakery that makes a product called The Good Kake. It has been on sale for four years and has established a good-sized market with growing sales. Alan is very health conscious and has devised a cake made exclusively from healthy ingredients that are believed to lead to a healthier outcome and a longer life. On the wrapping, Alan goes through the arguments as to why one should eat his product. "If you want a long life and to be as active as you can until the end, eating healthy will provide that. This Kake, made from healthy natural kale and other produce of the earth, untainted by artificial colours and sweeteners, can be a part of the balanced diet needed to extend the quality and length of our lives. Should be eaten as part of an overall organic healthy diet." Christina has begun selling a similar looking product in similar almost identical wrapping called Not the Good Kake! The wrapping replicates almost exactly that of the Good Kake. On the wrapping there is a similar list of arguments to that of the Good Kake, but it is in fact a counterargument, stating that an enjoyable life is better than a long life and how important it is to enjoy life rather than be in misery extending it through suffering tasteless food. Alan has noted that his sales, which had been booming have stabilised and even dropped a little since Not the Good Kake came on the market. In the meantime, Not the Good Kake has had a stunning sales success. Alan believes that people are confusing his product with hers. Advise Alan what action, if any, he might have against Christina in the law of tort.

questions.description: Amy works in a supermarket that opens 24 hours a day. Advise Amy as to what her legal position in the law of tort is…
questions.text: Amy works in a supermarket that opens 24 hours a day. She mostly takes the night shift as it pays better even though the hours are unsocial. During the summer months it is not too bad but as winter draws in, the long dark nights begin to take a toll and Amy is increasingly tired at the end of her shift. When leaving work, Amy is required to clock out and leave by a back alley where the rubbish bins are kept and make her way around to the front where her car is parked. As a result of the rising cost of electricity, management have decided to control their use of electricity and have taken a number of measures to reduce electricity usage. One is to replace the lighting where Amy has to leave sensor lighting only coming on when someone is in the alley. The other is to reduce the intensity of the lighting in the car park to a very low background level but which provided no real visibility. Unfortunately, the sensor lighting is slow to operate, and Amy often finds that she trips over things in the dark before the lights come on. She has complained to management who said they would look into it but nothing has happened. One night, as Amy is leaving, she trips over a crate and injured her ankle before the lights came on. Amy found it difficult to walk but continued to the front where her car park was parked. In the dark, she thought she could hear some muffled voices but continued as best she could. Suddenly from the darkness two youths attacked her and stole her handbag. Advise Amy as to what her legal position in the law of tort is with respect to her employers.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law relating to the impact of death in a tort action and suggest what, if any, reforms you think…
questions.text: Critically discuss the law relating to the impact of death in a tort action and suggest what, if any, reforms you think might be necessary.

questions.description: Marion is a surgeon working at First Memorial Hospital. Duty Of Care.
questions.text: Marion is a surgeon working at First Memorial Hospital. Following extensive consultations, Marion was to undertake a complicated surgery on Dave to remove some possibly cancerous cells. Her assessment at the time was that she would use the hospital's approved operating procedure for those sorts of cases, although she was aware that there was new research on these procedures. This research argued for a new alternative approach which produced better outcomes, and which was safer than that approved by the hospital. In fact, many hospitals around the world had incorporated this new procedure into their approved methods. While operating on Dave, she realised that he would be an ideal candidate for the new procedure that she was well able to undertake. In fact, she considered that it carried less risk than the approved method in this case. However, she was uncomfortable with departing from the hospital approved procedure in any situation, feeling that she would be better protected by staying within the rules, so she stuck to the approved procedure. Following the operation, Dave suffered some complications that could have been avoided had Marion followed the new procedure. The guidelines for procedures at the hospital stated that "a surgeon should operate within hospital approved guidelines unless using their professional judgement they form the opinion that a recognised alternative can be used for a better patient outcome." Advise Marion if, under the law of tort, she had breached her duty of care to Dave in these circumstances.

questions.description: Harry was giving a lecture on Greek mythology at a local college to a… Advise Robin if, under the law of tort, she has…
questions.text: Harry was giving a lecture on Greek mythology at a local college to a class of about 20 students. During the course of the lecture, an emergency siren activated in the class. Harry then proceeded to look at a text message on his phone. He then told the class that the college had informed him that someone had been seen on the campus with a gun and that an emergency response was being initiated. Harry told the class that the procedure was to lock the door to the room and for everyone to remain in the class but to sit under their desks or to take cover in, behind or under something. People were naturally panicked and began running around the place, although most followed Harry's orders and got under their desks. Harry went and locked the door and switched out the lights. At that point, Robin walked towards the door, but Harry told her that was too dangerous and for her to get under a desk and wait. Robin screamed that she needed to get home to her family as she had just received a text that her father had a heart attack. Harry was however adamant that no one could leave. Robin then returned to sit at her desk. When told by Harry to get under it, Robin said that she was not afraid of anyone, and they could "bring it on". Harry said she was putting everyone in danger and pulled her off the chair and pushed her under the desk. A few minutes later, Harry announced that it had been a drill to test the readiness of the college to deal with an emergency. Advise Robin if, under the law of tort, she has any action against Harry for his actions.

questions.description: Establishing a duty of care owed by the defendant has now been largely… Duty Of Care.
questions.text: Establishing a duty of care owed by the defendant has now been largely settled into specific categories beyond which the courts are reluctant to move. Critically discuss this statement with reference to the jurisprudence.
